content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
October 30, 1997
Councilmember Jeffrey Jandura
City of Lemon Grove
3232 Main Street
Lemon Grove, California 91945
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-477
Dear Councilmember Jandura:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions ofthe
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1. Will the California Department of Transportation be a disqualifying “source of
income” to you if payment is made through negotiation or after a court judgment involving your
real property?
2. May you participate in decisions involving the purchase of your real property by
CalTrans?
3. May the California Department of Transportation, by purchasing your property,
unilaterally prevent you from acting on matters involving that department?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Payment to you, either through negotiation or court judgment, from the State of
California Department of Transportation would be considered a disqualifying “source of
income.” However, under your facts, the public generally exception will apply.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 3 Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, CA 958l4.P. 0. Box 807, Sacramento, CA 95812. (916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-477
Page 2
2. You may not participate in any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material fmancial effect on any ofyour economic interests.
3. If the California Department of Transportation purchases your property, or becomes a
source of promised income, you may still participate in decisions involving that agency unless
there will be a material financial effect on one ofyour other economic interests. The public
generally exception may apply to the decisions ifthe exception applies to all ofyour economic
interests, including CalTrans.
FACTS
You are a councilmember in the City of Lemon Grove. The California Department of
Transportation (“CalTrans”) is constructing a freeway adjacent to, and in part, through the City
ofLemon Grove. The city has had continuing negotiations with CalTrans regarding the
relocation of a historical structure and revision of a freeway agreement.
You have been informed by CalTrans that they intend to appraise, and perhaps acquire
real property owned by you and your wife and rented to others. You inquire whether you may
make, participate in making, or influence any decisions involving the revision ofthe freeway
agreement which will affect your real property interests and your tenants. The decisions
involving the relocation of a historical structure will not affect your economic interests.
In a telephone conversation on October 9, 1997, you stated that CalTrans has now
appraised your property and plans to widen the street in order to build a freeway ramp. This
project will affect the back yard ofyour real property. You anticipate that negotiations will begin
shortly regarding CalTrans providing you with a monthly payment to supplement the decrease in
the rental value ofyour real property.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or using
their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to
know they have a financial interest.
An official has a fmancial interest in a governmental decision within the meaning of
Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material fmancial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a member of
the official’s immediate family, or on, among other things:


File No. A-97-477
Page 3
“(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct
or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more.
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by orpromised to the public official
within 12 monthsprior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position
of management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a
gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. The
amount ofthe value of gifts specified by this subdivision shall be
adjusted biennially by the commission to equal the same amount
determined by the commission pursuant to subdivision (f) of
Section 89503.2 (Section 87103, emphasis added.)
Finally, Regulation 18702.1 (a)(4) provides that an official may not participate in a
decision if it will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities ofthe official or
his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at least $250 in a 12-month period.
Regulation 18702.l(a)(4) does not apply to a fmancial effect on the value ofreal property owned
directly or indirectly by the official, or a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value
of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has an investment interest.
Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a Governmental Decision
A public official makes a governmental decision or participates in the making of a
governmental decision whenever the public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a
course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf ofthe agency. (Regulation
18700(b).) Additionally, a public official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:
2 The gift limit for the period January 1, 1997, through December31, 1998, is $290. (Regulation
18940.2.)


File No. A-97-477
Page 4
“(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a
governmental entity or private person regarding the decision; or
(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker,
either directly or without significant intervening substantive
review, by:
(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which
requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion,
orally or in writing, which requires the exercise ofjudgment on the
part ofthe official or designated employee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision.” (Regulation 18700(c).)
With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an official’s agency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control ofhis or her agency, an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant ofthe agency. Attempts to influence
include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer. Please note, however, that a public official is permitted to appear
before the city council in the same manner as any other member of the general public, solely to
represent himself or herself in a matter that is related to interests in real property wholly owned
by the public official or a member of his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18700.1.)
Accordingly, as a public official, if it is reasonably foreseeable that your economic
interests will be materially affected by a governmental decision, you must not only disqualify
yourself from participating in formal decisions ofthe city council which may affect such
interests, but you must also abstain from attempting to influence such decisions by
communicating with other members ofthe city council or with city staff regarding the decisions.
Economic Interests
a. The State of California as a Source of Income
You have stated that the State of California Department of Transportation has appraised
and intends to acquire certain real property owned by you and your wife which you use as rental
property. If the State of California acquires your real property or is a source ofpromised


File No. A-97-477
Page 5
income,
3 the State of California would become a source of income to you even if the property
was acquired through a court judgment. (Section 82030; Barbosa Advice Letter, No. 1-91-365.)
b. Tenants as a Source of Income
Ifyour tenant has been a source of income to you of $250 or more in the twelve months
preceding a decision, your tenant is an economic interest of yours. (Section 87103(c).)
c. Real Property Interests
You and your wife own real property which we can assume you have an investment worth
$1,000 or more. Therefore, your real property is an economic interest ofyours. (Section
87103(b).)
If a decision before you will affect your real property interests or your sources of income,
you would have to disqualify yourself from participating in decisions that affect that interest.
Foreseeability
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. To be foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere possibility;
however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm.
(1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-991; Witty. Morrow (1977)70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest; it
seeks to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at
823.)
It is foreseeable that decisions regarding the relocation of the historical structure and the
placement ofthe freeway will have an effect on your economic interests. You have an interest in
real property located in the area. It is reasonably foreseeable that these changes in the character
of the area surrounding the real property in which you have an economic interest will affect the
value ofyour real property. Moreover, the projected changes might increase or decrease the
value ofyour rental property thus having an economic effect on your tenant. It is also
foreseeable that CalTrans would be affected. Accordingly, ifthe effect ofthe proposed decisions
will be material, you must disqualify yourself from participating in such decisions.
In some situations, income may be promised to a public official, but not yet actually received by the
official. In such situations, the source of promised income is also a fmancial interest of the public official; in other
words, the source of promised income does not escape characterization as a financial interest ofthe promisee/public
official just because the income has not yet been delivered. (Krause Advice Letter, No. A-97-40 1.)


File No. A-97-477
Page 6
Materiality
The initial step in assessing the materiality ofthe financial effect of a governmental
decision requires a determination as to whether the involvement of the official’s economic
interest is direct or indirect. Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining whether
an official’s economic interest is materially affected by a decision. If the official’s economic
interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1 provides the appropriate standard
for assessing materiality.
With respect to sources of income in your case, Regulation 18702.1(b) states that a
person or business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official’s agency when that
person or entity, either personally or by an agent:
“(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official’s agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if
a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business entity.”
This test will apply if CalTrans becomes a source of income or promised income to you
and a decision before the city council involves CalTrans in one ofthe manners discussed above.
This test may apply to your tenants also.
To determine ifyour real property is directly involved in a decision, you must apply
Regulation 18702.1 (a)(3) which provides:
(a) The effect of a decision is material if any ofthe following
applies:
***
(3) Interest in Real Property-
(A) The decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in
or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of real property in which the official has
a direct or indirect interest (other than a leasehold interest) of
$1,000 or more, or a similar decision affecting such property;
(B) The decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement
authorizing a specific use or uses of such property;


File No. A-97-477
Page 7
(C) The decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on such
property; or
(D) The decision is to designate the survey area, to select
the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project
area committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt
the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area,
or to rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real
property in which the official has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) ofthe
redevelopment area.
(E) For purposes of this subdivision, the terms “zoning”
and “rezoning” shall refer to the act of establishing or changing the
zoning or land use designation on the subject property. The terms
“zoning” and “rezoning” shall not refer to an amendment of an
existing zoning ordinance or other land use regulation (such as
changes in the uses permitted, or development standards
applicable, within a particular zoning category) which is applicable
to all other properties designated in that category, which shall be
analyzed under Title 2, Division 6, Section 18702.3(c) of the
California Code of Regulations.”
Ifyour economic interests are indirectly involved in a decision, the appropriate standards
for determining materiality are those of Regulations 18702.2-18702.6. As to your tenants,
Regulation 18702.6 states that the effect of a decision is material as to an individual who is a
source of income to an official and is indirectly impacted by a decision if any ofthe following
applies:
“(a) The decision will affect the individual’s income,
investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or
(b) The decision will affect the individual’s real property
interest in a manner that is considered material under Section
18702.3 or Section 18702.4.”
To assist you in making this determination, we are enclosing copies of Regulations
18702.3 and 18702.4. Please note that the standards in these regulations must be applied to the
economic interests of your tenant as to each decision.
Finally, Regulation 18702.3 applies ifyour property is indirectly involved in a decision.
We are unable to advise you which regulations apply. You must apply them based on the facts
pertaining to each governmental decision.


File No. A-97-477
Page 8
Public Generally Exception
Even if the reasonably foreseeable fmancial effect of a decision is material as to any of
your interests, disqualification is required only if the effect is distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally. (Section 87103.) Regulation 18703 provides:
“(a) General Rule: Except as provided in Government Code
Sections 87102.6 and 87103.5, the material financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official’s economic interests is
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if both
subdivisions (a)(l) and (a)(2) of this regulation apply:
(1) Significant Segment: The governmental decision will
affect a “significant segment” of the public generally as set forth
below:
(A) For decisions that affect the official’s economic interests
(excluding interests in a business entity which are analyzed under
subdivision (B)):
(i) Ten percent or more ofthe population in the jurisdiction of
the official’s agency or the district the official represents, or
(ii) Ten percent or more of all property owners, all home
owners, or all households in the jurisdiction ofthe official’s agency
or the district the official represents, or
(B) For decisions that affect a business entity in which the
official has an interest as set forth in Section 87103, fifty percent of
all businesses in the jurisdiction or the district the official
represents, so long as the segment is composed ofpersons other
than a single industry, trade, or profession; or,
(C) For decisions that affect any ofthe official’s economic
interests, the decision will affect 5,000 individuals who are
residents ofthe jurisdiction; or,
(D) The decision will affect a segment of the population which
does not meet any of the standards in subdivisions (a)(1)(A)
through (a)(1)(C), however, due to exceptional circumstances
regarding the decision, it is determined such segment constitutes a
significant segment of the public generally.
(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The governmental
decision will affect the official’s economic interest in substantially
the sanie maimer as it will affect the significant segment identified
in subdivision (a)(1) of this regulation.”
Because the California Department of Transportation is an agency ofthe State of
California, if the effect of a decision is material as to the state agency, the effect of the decision
flows to the State of California. (Douglas Advice Letter, No. I-90-128A; Howard Advice Letter,
A-92-105.) Therefore, the effect of the decision would be the same as the effect on the public
generally. Accordingly, unless the decision will have a material fmancial effect on your real
property interest or on your tenants, no disqualification is required based solely on the payments
you expect to receive from the State of California or after you receive such payments.


File No. A-97.-477
Page 9
Bifurcation
Ifyou are disqualified due to a material financial effect on your real property or your
tenants, you should consider the following. Under certain circumstances, large and complex
decisions may be divided into separate decisions when an official has a disqualifying economic
interest in one component ofthe decisions which is not interdependent upon other components.
However, under some circumstances, a series of decisions may be too interrelated to be
considered separately. (Miller Advice Letter, No. A-82-l 19.) This would be the case, for
example, if a decision on one aspect of the freeway agreement would substantially affect a
decision on another aspect ofthe agreement that impacts your property. Under such
circumstances, if you must disqualify yourself from participating in one of the decisions, you
must also disqualify yourselffrom participating in the other related decisions.
However, ifyour determine that it is possible to consider one particular aspect ofthe
revision of the freeway agreement independently of the others, and ifthe items are not
interrelated, they may be bifurcated and considered separately from other elements ofthe revision
agreement. In order to bifurcate certain issues, the city council must adhere to the following
procedure:
1. The decisions in which you have a disqualifying economic interest must be segregated
from other decisions.
2. The decisions from which you are disqualified must be considered first, and a final
decision must be reached by the city council without your participation.
3. Once a final decision has been reached on a particular item, you may participate in the
deliberations and vote regarding other areas ofthe revision ofthe freeway agreement as long as
those deliberations and vote do not affect the previous decisions from which you were
disqualified. (MerkuloffAdvice Letter, No. 1-90-542; McNatt Advice Letter, No. 1-90-174;
Howard Advice Letter, supra.)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Lynda Doherty
Consultant, Legal Division
SGC:LD:jlw
Enclosures

sections.question:
1. Will the California Department of Transportation be a disqualifying “source of
income” to you if payment is made through negotiation or after a court judgment involving your
real property?
2. May you participate in decisions involving the purchase of your real property by
CalTrans?
3. May the California Department of Transportation, by purchasing your property,
unilaterally prevent you from acting on matters involving that department?

income.” However, under your facts, the public generally exception will apply.428 3 Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, CA 958l4.P. 0. Box 807, Sacramento, CA 95812. (916) 322-5660
2. You may not participate in any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material fmancial effect on any ofyour economic interests.
3. If the California Department of Transportation purchases your property, or becomes a
source of promised income, you may still participate in decisions involving that agency unless
there will be a material financial effect on one ofyour other economic interests. The public
generally exception may apply to the decisions ifthe exception applies to all ofyour economic
interests, including CalTrans.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or using
their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to
know they have a financial interest.
An official has a fmancial interest in a governmental decision within the meaning of
Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material fmancial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a member of
the official’s immediate family, or on, among other things:
“(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct
or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more.
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by orpromised to the public official
within 12 monthsprior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position
of management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a
gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. The
amount ofthe value of gifts specified by this subdivision shall be
adjusted biennially by the commission to equal the same amount
determined by the commission pursuant to subdivision (f) of
Section 89503.2 (Section 87103, emphasis added.)
Finally, Regulation 18702.1 (a)(4) provides that an official may not participate in a
decision if it will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities ofthe official or
his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at least $250 in a 12-month period.
Regulation 18702.l(a)(4) does not apply to a fmancial effect on the value ofreal property owned
directly or indirectly by the official, or a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value
of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has an investment interest.
Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a Governmental Decision
A public official makes a governmental decision or participates in the making of a
governmental decision whenever the public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a
course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf ofthe agency. (Regulation
18700(b).) Additionally, a public official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:
2 The gift limit for the period January 1, 1997, through December31, 1998, is $290. (Regulation
18940.2.)
“(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a
governmental entity or private person regarding the decision; or
(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker,
either directly or without significant intervening substantive
review, by:
(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which
requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion,
orally or in writing, which requires the exercise ofjudgment on the
part ofthe official or designated employee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision.” (Regulation 18700(c).)
With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an official’s agency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control ofhis or her agency, an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant ofthe agency. Attempts to influence
include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer. Please note, however, that a public official is permitted to appear
before the city council in the same manner as any other member of the general public, solely to
represent himself or herself in a matter that is related to interests in real property wholly owned
by the public official or a member of his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18700.1.)
Accordingly, as a public official, if it is reasonably foreseeable that your economic
interests will be materially affected by a governmental decision, you must not only disqualify
yourself from participating in formal decisions ofthe city council which may affect such
interests, but you must also abstain from attempting to influence such decisions by
communicating with other members ofthe city council or with city staff regarding the decisions.
Economic Interests
a. The State of California as a Source of Income
You have stated that the State of California Department of Transportation has appraised
and intends to acquire certain real property owned by you and your wife which you use as rental
property. If the State of California acquires your real property or is a source ofpromised
income,
3 the State of California would become a source of income to you even if the property
was acquired through a court judgment. (Section 82030; Barbosa Advice Letter, No. 1-91-365.)
b. Tenants as a Source of Income
Ifyour tenant has been a source of income to you of $250 or more in the twelve months
preceding a decision, your tenant is an economic interest of yours. (Section 87103(c).)
c. Real Property Interests
You and your wife own real property which we can assume you have an investment worth
$1,000 or more. Therefore, your real property is an economic interest ofyours. (Section
87103(b).)
If a decision before you will affect your real property interests or your sources of income,
you would have to disqualify yourself from participating in decisions that affect that interest.

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. To be foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere possibility;
however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm.

(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest; it
seeks to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at
823.)
It is foreseeable that decisions regarding the relocation of the historical structure and the

official. In such situations, the source of promised income is also a fmancial interest of the public official; in other
words, the source of promised income does not escape characterization as a financial interest ofthe promisee/public
official just because the income has not yet been delivered. (Krause Advice Letter, No. A-97-40 1.)

The initial step in assessing the materiality ofthe financial effect of a governmental
decision requires a determination as to whether the involvement of the official’s economic

“(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official’s agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if
a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business entity.”
This test will apply if CalTrans becomes a source of income or promised income to you
and a decision before the city council involves CalTrans in one ofthe manners discussed above.
This test may apply to your tenants also.

(a) The effect of a decision is material if any ofthe following

income” to you if payment is made through negotiation or after a court judgment involving your
real property?
2. May you participate in decisions involving the purchase of your real property by
CalTrans?
3. May the California Department of Transportation, by purchasing your property,
unilaterally prevent you from acting on matters involving that department?

CONCLUSION: 1. Payment to you, either through negotiation or court judgment, from the State of
California Department of Transportation would be considered a disqualifying “source of
income.” However, under your facts, the public generally exception will apply.428 3 Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, CA 958l4.P. 0. Box 807, Sacramento, CA 95812. (916) 322-5660
2. You may not participate in any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material fmancial effect on any ofyour economic interests.
3. If the California Department of Transportation purchases your property, or becomes a
source of promised income, you may still participate in decisions involving that agency unless
there will be a material financial effect on one ofyour other economic interests. The public
generally exception may apply to the decisions ifthe exception applies to all ofyour economic
interests, including CalTrans.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION October 30, 1997 Councilmember Jeffrey Jandura City of Lemon Grove 3232 Main Street Lemon Grove, California 91945 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-97-477 Dear Councilmember Jandura: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions ofthe Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ QUESTIONS 1. Will the California Department of Transportation be a disqualifying “source of income” to you if payment is made through negotiation or after a court judgment involving your real property? 2. May you participate in decisions involving the purchase of your real property by CalTrans? 3. May the California Department of Transportation, by purchasing your property, unilaterally prevent you from acting on matters involving that department? CONCLUSIONS 1. Payment to you, either through negotiation or court judgment, from the State of California Department of Transportation would be considered a disqualifying “source of income.” However, under your facts, the public generally exception will apply. Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations. 428 3 Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, CA 958l4.P. 0. Box 807, Sacramento, CA 95812. (916) 322-5660 File No. A-97-477 Page 2 2. You may not participate in any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable and material fmancial effect on any ofyour economic interests. 3. If the California Department of Transportation purchases your property, or becomes a source of promised income, you may still participate in decisions involving that agency unless there will be a material financial effect on one ofyour other economic interests. The public generally exception may apply to the decisions ifthe exception applies to all ofyour economic interests, including CalTrans. FACTS You are a councilmember in the City of Lemon Grove. The California Department of Transportation (“CalTrans”) is constructing a freeway adjacent to, and in part, through the City ofLemon Grove. The city has had continuing negotiations with CalTrans regarding the relocation of a historical structure and revision of a freeway agreement. You have been informed by CalTrans that they intend to appraise, and perhaps acquire real property owned by you and your wife and rented to others. You inquire whether you may make, participate in making, or influence any decisions involving the revision ofthe freeway agreement which will affect your real property interests and your tenants. The decisions involving the relocation of a historical structure will not affect your economic interests. In a telephone conversation on October 9, 1997, you stated that CalTrans has now appraised your property and plans to widen the street in order to build a freeway ramp. This project will affect the back yard ofyour real property. You anticipate that negotiations will begin shortly regarding CalTrans providing you with a monthly payment to supplement the decrease in the rental value ofyour real property.
